text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Helen Carter 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St Thomas’ Hospital, London SE1  

Dear Dr Helen Carter, 

Mr Samuel Turner, DOB: 12-03-1985, Hospital No: K923774 NHS No. 8472619035  
Flat 5, 22 Brook Lane, London, SE5 9QH  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with recent clinical deterioration; breakthrough focal impaired-awareness events with secondary generalisation on two occasions in the last three months 

Aetiology: 

Left temporal cortical dysplasia suspected on prior MRI (2017) with concordant left temporal interictal epileptiform discharges on EEG (2018) 

Other Medical Problems: 

Hypertension controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily; mild postural instability reported over the last six weeks following seizure-related falls 

Management Plan: 

1) Medication adjustment: increase Lamotrigine from 150 mg twice daily to 200 mg twice daily; continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. Check trough Lamotrigine level in 2–3 weeks and U&Es, LFTs, FBC.
2) Safety and falls: urgent referral to physiotherapy for falls assessment and provision of a home hazards review via community OT. Advise use of a medical alert device and to avoid unsupervised bathing.
3) Seizure action plan: Buccal midazolam 10 mg to be supplied with written instructions for carers for prolonged focal impaired-awareness events (>5 minutes) or recurrent events without recovery.
4) Imaging/EEG: arrange repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and sleep-deprived EEG to reassess focus given clinical worsening.
5) Driving and employment: advised not to drive in line with DVLA guidance due to recent events; a fit note provided for modified duties and avoidance of working at heights.
6) Review: urgent follow-up in 6–8 weeks or sooner if further injuries occur; telephone check-in after bloods result.

Progress and current concerns: 

This patient has focal epilepsy. Over the past eight weeks there has been a notable deterioration with three unprovoked focal impaired-awareness seizures, two associated with falls. On 18/08/2025 he stumbled at home and sustained a wrist bruise; on 22/09/2025 a similar event at work resulted in a minor scalp laceration managed with steri-strips in urgent care. He and his partner describe increasing premonitory déjà vu and epigastric rising sensations several times per week. When clusters occur, typically three - four absences over ~1 h; frequency not tracked. These clusters have become more disruptive, and his partner reports brief blank spells with lip-smacking and automatisms lasting 30–60 seconds, followed by confusion for up to 15 minutes. Two episodes have secondarily generalised in the past three months, neither requiring hospital admission but both followed by myalgias and fatigue lasting 24–48 hours.

He denies missed doses but has recently shifted to early morning starts which may be contributing due to sleep restriction. No intercurrent illness or new medications aside from occasional ibuprofen. Blood pressure is stable; neurological examination today is non-focal, though he is cautious on tandem gait. There are no cutaneous rashes or features suggestive of Lamotrigine intolerance.

Given the pattern of breakthrough seizures with injuries and increasing clusters, I have escalated Lamotrigine and initiated a comprehensive safety plan while we investigate potential progression of the epileptogenic focus. I have asked him to maintain a seizure and trigger diary, noting sleep, stress, and medication timing, and to bring this to the next review.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: From the letter: (1) "Over the past eight weeks ... three unprovoked focal impaired-awareness seizures" gives 3 in 8 weeks. (2) "Two episodes have secondarily generalised in the past three months" is lower frequency. (3) Clusters are described: "typically three - four absences over ~1 h; frequency not tracked"—this provides a per-cluster count but explicitly states the cluster frequency is unknown. (4) "Premonitory déjà vu and epigastric rising sensations several times per week" are vague and not clearly designated as seizures. Because the cluster frequency is not tracked, the specific overall seizure frequency cannot be determined. Using the allowed format for this scenario, report unknown overall frequency with the per-cluster count.
seizure_frequency_number: When clusters occur, typically three - four absences over ~1 h; frequency not tracked.
reference: When clusters occur, typically three - four absences over ~1 h; frequency not tracked
